coredata.dc:title: Preparation of room-temperature phosphorescence fluorine–nitrogen Co-doped carbon dots with sodium lignosulfonate for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chenyang
Guo, Jiandong
Kong, Fanwei
Zou, Shuqi
Wu, Mingyang
Peng, Jinsong
Li, Xiaobai
Chen, Chunxia
Ma, Hongwei
coredata.dc:description: The key to achieving room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) is to narrow the energy gap between the first singlet state (S1) and first triplet state (T1) and to stabilize the triplet excited state. In this paper, an environmentally friendly fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared based on sodium lignosulfonate. The films emit stable green phosphorescence at room temperature due to the hydrogen bond formed between carbon dots (CDs) and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), and the heavy-atom effect of fluorine atoms promotes intersystem crossing (ISC). Furthermore, the n-π∗ electron transitions of the CN/C–N bond also promote the green phosphorescence emission of FNCDs@PVA. Compared with single nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), the steric protection provided by C–F and hydrogen bonds within FNCDs can help the phosphorescence lifetime to reach 494 ms. Based on the properties of FNCDs under different pH, temperature and humidity, we propose several multidimensional advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room temperature phosphorescence
Fluorine-nitrogen co-doped
Carbon dots
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of FNCDs and NCDs. (a) Synthesis of FNCDs@PVA films. (b–d) TEM images of (b) NCDs, (c) FNCDs-1, (d) FNCDs-3; inset: HR-TEM lattice fringe images of CDs. (e–g) Size distribution of (e) NCDs, (f) FNCDs-1, (g) FNCDs-3. (h–j) XPS spectra of (h) NCDs, (i) FNCDs-1, (j) FNCDs-3.

(a) FT-IR spectrogram. (b) Schematic diagram of the formation of hydrogen bonds of CDs in PVA solution; Inset: FNCDs-1@PVA Stereo-blossom under UV light on/off. (c–e) Fluorescence spectra of solution corresponding to (c) NCDs, (d) FNCDs-1, (e) FNCDs-3. (f–h) Fluorescence spectra of different films; Inset: CD@PVA film images in daylight and under UV light.

Phosphorescent properties of CDs@PVA RTP films. (a–c) Phosphorescence emission spectra of the films based on (a) NCDs@PVA, (b) FNCDs-1@PVA, (c) FNCDs-3@PVA at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@PVA. (e) pH-responsive fluorescence spectra of CDs@PVA. (f) pH-responsive phosphorescence spectrum of CDs@PVA. (g) FNCDs-1@PVA fluorescence excitation (violet), fluorescence emission (light violet), phosphorescence excitation (blue), and phosphorescence emission (light blue) spectra. (h) Maps of energy level transitions within the film.
(a) Digital encryption based on film lifetime. (b) RAS digital signature encryption strategy.
(a, b) (a)Encryption (b) decryption strategies for programmed 3D stereo information.

head.ce:title: Preparation of room-temperature phosphorescence fluorine–nitrogen Co-doped carbon dots with sodium lignosulfonate for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Complex Traits and Protein Machines in Organisms, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Complex Traits and Protein Machines in Organisms

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Heilongjiang Key Laboratory of Complex Traits and Protein Machines in Organisms, Harbin 150040, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chenyang Zhang and Jiandong Guo contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The key to achieving room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) is to narrow the energy gap between the first singlet state (S1) and first triplet state (T1) and to stabilize the triplet excited state. In this paper, an environmentally friendly fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were prepared based on sodium lignosulfonate. The films emit stable green phosphorescence at room temperature due to the hydrogen bond formed between carbon dots (CDs) and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), and the heavy-atom effect of fluorine atoms promotes intersystem crossing (ISC). Furthermore, the n-π∗ electron transitions of the CN/C–N bond also promote the green phosphorescence emission of FNCDs@PVA. Compared with single nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), the steric protection provided by C–F and hydrogen bonds within FNCDs can help the phosphorescence lifetime to reach 494 ms. Based on the properties of FNCDs under different pH, temperature and humidity, we propose several multidimensional advanced information encryption and anti-counterfeiting strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the problem of information security and anti-counterfeiting has received much attention because of its enormous impact on the economy and military [1–4]. With the continuous progress of science and technology, society has higher requirements for the security and capacity of information storage. Therefore, more and more people are committed to developing high-level security devices to achieve information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, such as fluorescent/phosphorescent ink, encoding and polarization security labels [5–12]. In addition, CDs with excellent luminous intensity can be used to prepare light-emitting diodes (LEDs), which have broad application value [13]. However, converting pure organic or organometallic materials into optical devices is complicated and requires high costs, inevitably causing environmental hazards [14,15]. Due to their good photoluminescent properties, low toxicity, and low production cost, CDs are considered to have great potential for development in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting [16–19]. Moreover, the emergence of RTP materials breaks the notion that only stable phosphorescence can be emitted at cryogenic temperatures, largely promoting the research on phosphorescent materials. Compared with traditional fluorescent CDs, the new RTP CDs are more ideal encryption materials; they can briefly eliminate the interference of background fluorescence and also have longer luminescence lifetimes and longer emission wavelengths.

The formation of RTP CDs is challenging due to the spin-forbidden nature and non-radiative vibration. Therefore, making a large overlap between the S1 and T1 energy layers and suppressing the vibration/rotation of the triplet exciton is the most effective way to achieve RTP. To date, there have been many reports on RTP CDs; although RTP CDs have excellent biocompatibility and optical properties, most of them also have non-negligible problems [20–22]. Firstly, some pure organics can achieve the preparation of long-lived phosphorescent materials, but their toxicity and cost are much higher than that of CDs [23]. Secondly, it is difficult to generate efficient RTP emission because the energy of excited triplet states of organic molecules is easily quenched by exposure to oxygen through thermodynamic vibration or collision and different environmental changes, resulting in short luminescence lifetimes, insensitive and unstable devices [24–29]. Therefore, a more stable, long luminescence lifetime and environmentally friendly RTP CDs are urgently needed.

As a by-product of the industrial pulping process, sodium lignosulfonate, with its excellent degradability and green nature has been emphasized in many fields, such as porous carbon materials for capacitors and environmentally friendly flame-retardant materials [30,31]. Therefore, it contributes greatly to helping to reduce waste generation and energy consumption. In addition, its good water solubility broadens its application in industry and manufacturing, and has a good development prospect. PVA is a hydroxyl-rich polymer, and its chain structure inhibits the vibration/rotation of triplet excitons through the formation of hydrogen bonds with CDs to achieve isolation from the aqueous environment, which is very helpful for improving the stability of CDs and prolonging the lifetime of phosphorescence [32,33]. In addition to common single-element doping (nitrogen doping or phosphorus doping), two-element doping can generate new energy levels and promote the ISC process of the CDs system, which is manifested in a longer phosphorescence lifetime and more stable steric structure [15,34–37]. The C–F bonding and the large number of intra/inter-dot hydrogen bonds in the PVA environment can provide a good steric structure to protect the RTP from oxygen quenching at room temperature. In addition, the n-π∗ electron transitions of CN/C–N bonds can also promote ISC. Inspired by the above ideas, we prepared flexible phosphorescent films of RTP FNCDs by choosing environmentally friendly sodium lignosulfonate as a precursor and Et3N•3HF as a fluorine-nitrogen co-doping reagent. By comparing the mono-nitrogen-doped NCDs@PVA with FNCDs@PVA, it was found that the phosphorescence lifetime of FNCDs@PVA was prolonged by 21 ms. FNCDs@PVA showed that the naked eye could observe green RTP properties and obvious green phosphorescence for 3 s after removing UV light. Sodium lignosulfonate is a biomass material with great potential for application, and it is interesting to explore different environmental conditions for RTP CD materials made from it. Thus, in this work, we explored the effects of temperature, humidity, and pH on the properties of this RTP CD and focused on explaining the reasons for the phosphorescence changes due to pH variations. It is worth noting that the phosphorescence intensity of RTP CDs@PVA decreased slightly with decreasing pH for a certain period, which was attributed to the breaking of hydrogen bonds by protonation. Conversely, with increasing alkalinity, deprotonation caused the broken hydrogen bonds to be compounded and more hydrogen bonds to be generated, which increased the phosphorescence intensity [38]. Based on the above characteristics, we apply NCDs and FNCDs RTP flexible films in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. Therefore, this study not only provides a wide range of green and low-cost materials for the production of high-quality RTP CDs, but also works to convert biomass waste into high-value products. By combining temporal scale, spatial scale, and computer algorithms, we achieve multi-dimensional protection of high-level information while expanding the storage capacity of information.

PVA (Mw 77092–79294; 87–89 % hydrolyzed; ω ≧ 99.0 %), Et3N•3HF (ω = 98 %), sodium lignosulfonate (ω = 96 %), urea (ω ≧ 99.0 %), qualitative filter paper (Model number: 102; Filtration speed: medium speed; Pore size: 20 μm). Deionized water was used in the experiments. Unless otherwise stated, all reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

Synthesis of NCDs: NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Urea (2.0 g) and sodium lignosulphonate (500 mg) were added to 15 mL of deionized water, stirred homogeneously and then heated in a reactor at 190 °C for 12 h. A brown dispersion was obtained after hydrothermal carbonation, which was pumped and filtered using a Büchner funnel and freeze-dried to a brown powder.

Synthesis of FNCDs-1: A hydrothermal method was used to synthesize FNCDs-1. Sodium lignosulfonate (500 mg) was added to 20 mL of deionized water, and then 1 mL of Et3N•3HF was stirred uniformly and heated in a reactor at 190 °C for 12 h. A yellow dispersion was obtained after hydrothermal carbonation, which was extracted by filtration using a Büchner funnel and freeze-dried into a brown powder.

Synthesis of FNCDs-3: FNCDs-3 was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Sodium lignosulfonate (500 mg) was added to 18 mL of deionized water, and then 3 mL of Et3N•3HF was stirred well and then heated in a reactor at 190 °C for 12 h. A yellow dispersion was obtained by hydrothermal carbonation, which was pumped through a Büchner funnel and freeze-dried into a brown powder.

Synthesis of CD@PVA flexible phosphorescent film. CDs@PVA (2 mg mL−1) was obtained by dissolving 2 mg of CD powder in 0.5 mL of water, adding 4 mL of 5 wt% PVA solution, mixing it well and placing it flat on a Petri dish, and waiting for the drying to obtain flexible RTP film.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were detected by a PU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). FT-IR spectra were recorded by a Nicolet iS10 infrared spectrometer from 500 cm−1–4000 cm−1 (Thermo Fisher, USA). Fluorescence spectra were measured using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Phosphorescence spectra were determined on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Temperature-dependent phosphorescence spectra and lifetimes were determined on an Edinburgh FLS 980 instrument with a microsecond flash-lamp (uF2). All photos were taken by a HUAWEI Mate 40 mobile phone under ambient conditions.

By varying the dopant reagent in the CDs, two other sets of CDs were prepared simultaneously as controls, NCDs without fluorine and FNCDs-3 with more fluorine-nitrogen reagent content. Therefore, these three sets of CDs were named NCDs, FNCDs-1, and FNCDs-3, respectively. FNCDs-1 were prepared via a traditional hydrothermal method using sodium lignosulfonate as the carbon source, Et3N•3HF as a fluorine and nitrogen source (Fig. 1 (a)). The NCDs were prepared by replacing the Et3N•3HF with fluorine-free urea, with the carbon source and the ambient conditions remained unchanged. Sodium lignosulfonate has excellent water solubility compared to lignin; the mildness, high activity, and effectiveness of Et3N•3HF provide successful doping of F and N atoms. In addition, doping N atoms facilitates the formation of hydrogen bonds between FNCDs and solvent molecules; the C–F bonds formed by doping F atoms provide steric hindrance, contributing to the emission of RTP. To determine the origin of luminescence behaviors, the three types of RTP CDs were individually structural characterization analyses. As observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, the particle sizes of these three CDs were evenly distributed with good dispersion. The high-resolution (HR) TEM lattice fringe images (Fig. 1(b–d)) show the well-resolved lattice fringes with NCDs, FNCDs-1, and FNCDs-3 spacing were 0.17 nm, 0.18 nm, and 0.19 nm, respectively, which attributed to the typical (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [4,39,40]. Typically, the bandgap of CDs is influenced by several factors, such as its size, shape, surface structure, etc. The small lattice spacing of CDs results in a small size, and the bandgap increases with decreasing size [41–43]. Also, the average diameters of the spherical particles were ≈2.87 nm, 2.43 nm and 3.65 nm (Fig. 1(e–g)), the standard deviation of the particle size were 0.81, 0.37, and 0.89, respectively. According to the standard deviation of (Fig. 1(e–g)), the size distribution of FNCDs-1 is more homogeneous compared to NCDs and FNCDs-3. This indicates that FNCDs-1 has fewer surface state defects and avoids surface defects due to inhomogeneous growth, making it possible for surface defects to act as energy traps, thus suppressing the nonradiative relaxation process and preventing diminished luminescence.

The elemental compositions of the three CDs were measured by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The results showed that the three CDs have three identical peaks at 285.08 eV, 401.08 eV, and 532.08 eV, which correspond to the binding energies of C1s, N1s, and O1s; the main difference lies in the characteristic peaks of F1s (Fig. 1(h–j)). According to the XPS high-resolution spectra of NCDs, there are three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.7 eV for C1s, which correspond to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO bonds; the high-resolution spectra of N1s show the presence of C–N and amide/amino-type N atoms at 399.7 eV and 401.5 eV (Fig. S1). For FNCDs-1, three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.6 eV were observed for the high-resolution spectra of CC, C–N/C–O, and C–F, respectively; the high-resolution spectra of the N1s exhibited the presence of CN bonds and amide/amino type N atoms at 399.7 eV and 401.4 eV, where CN bonds are thought to promote RTP emission and amide/amino N atoms promote hydrogen bonding; the high-resolution spectra of the O1s showed the presence of CO (531.58 eV) and C–O (532.8 eV); high-resolution spectra of F1s revealed the presence of semi-ionic C–F bonds (684.4 eV), covalent C–F bonds (685.7 eV), and –CF2 bonds (686.6 eV) (Fig. S2). Among them, the presence of C–F bonds provide steric hindrance to RTP FNCDs, prevents the quenching of triplet excitons, and effectively promotes the RTP [4,15,28], the high bond energy of –CF2 makes FNCDs-1 more stable. Comparing FNCDs-3 with FNCDs-1, we found that they have very similar high-resolution spectra, with the obvious difference that FNCDs-3 does not show the characteristic peaks related to the –CF2 bond (Fig. S3). In addition, we confirmed the accuracy of the previously described XPS data by obtaining Fourier-transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the three CDs (Fig. 2 (a)). Firstly, the three CDs have broad absorption bands from 3700 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of the –OH group. At 1710 cm−1, CO stretching vibration is caused by sp2 conjugation. The C–O absorption peak is at 1035 cm−1. Meanwhile, nitrogen absorption peaks also can be observed, such as the CN bond at 1617 cm−1 and the N–H bond of amino/amide at 1520 cm−1 and 741 cm−1. It is worth noting that for NCDs, the C–NH shown at 1456 cm−1 confirmed the successful doping of N atoms in CDs, rather than the hydrogen bond that is formed [44]. Moreover, the stretching vibrations of a characteristic C–F are located at 1207 cm−1. These results are consistent with the XPS data we obtained.

Fig. 2(b) shows the bonding schematic of CDs in PVA solution. The CDs' fluorescence Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) spectra proved the three CDs@PVA's fluorescence color was blue (Fig. S4). Under the condition of an aqueous solution (2.0 mg mL−1), NCDs, FNCDs-1, and FNCDs-3 also showed blue fluorescence (Fig. S5). The optimal excitation wavelength of NCDs was 380 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelengths of FNCDs-1 and FNCDs-3 were 300 nm, showing excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 2(c–e)). Based on the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the three RTP CDs@PVA, the spectra showed π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of the C–N/CN bonds in the absorption region of 250–300 nm. As for the UV–Vis spectra of FNCDs-1 and FNCDs-3, the weak absorption peaks at 300–400 nm may be caused by the n-π∗ transition of C–F bonds, while for NCDs, the weak absorption peaks in this region may be assigned to the n-π∗ transition of CO or C–N (Fig. S6) [4,28,45]. The fluorescence spectroscopy of CDs@PVA can also be measured. Comparing the excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra in the aqueous state with those under CDs@PVA, we found that the maximum emission centers of the three CDs@PVA were 451, 437, 419 nm, respectively, and the optimal excitation wavelengths were 340 nm, which were inconsistent with aqueous CDs (Fig. 2(f–h)). This may be due to the fact that in an aqueous solution, free-moving CDs tend to aggregate over time, resulting in the attenuation of the surface molecular emission, which shows long-wavelength fluorescence. In contrast, the abundant hydrogen bonding between CDs and PVA chains produces bound CDs with stable surface state contributions corresponding to short wavelength emission [46].

Subsequently, the phosphorescent properties of CDs@PVA were investigated. The flexible films of NCDs@PVA, FNCDs-1@PVA, and FNCDs-3@PVA exhibited intense and long-lived green phosphorescence in atmospheric oxygen after turning off the UV lamp at room temperature. In this process, PVA, as an auxiliary substrate, provides an excellent steric structure for CDs. Hydroxyl groups on PVA generate hydrogen bonds with CDs, which inhibit the vibration and rotation of the triplet excitons, reduce the energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, and isolate the quenching of the triplet excitons by oxygen to a certain degree, which promotes the phosphorescence of CDs@PVA [32]. When the excitation wavelength was 260 nm, the strongest RTP emission was observed at wavelengths of 450 nm, 480 nm, and 480 nm (Fig. 3 (a–c)). Fig. 3(a) shows that the phosphorescence spectrum of NCDs@PVA is red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength, which may be due to the presence of multiple emission centers. Whereas Fig. 3(b, c) shows the phosphorescence spectra hardly change with the change of excitation wavelength, indicating that there is only one emission center. Different emission centers have different electronic structures and energy level distributions, which may contribute to the different phosphorescence lifetimes of NCDs@PVA and FNCDs@PVA. According to (Fig. 3(a–c)), it is shown that NCDs@PVA exhibit a clear excitation-dependent behavior, while FNCDs-1@PVA and FNCDs-3@PVA exhibit a weaker excitation-dependent behavior. This is related to multiple surface state defects such as nitrogen, fluorine, etc. and the weaker excitation wavelength dependence proves that FNCDs have fewer surface state defects [39,47,48]. Whereas, the phosphorescence properties are weakened by the introduction of more electron traps due to excessive fluorine doping. The phosphorescence CIE spectra show that CDs@PVA emitted green phosphorescence (Fig. S7). It is noteworthy that FNCDs-1@PVA have a long phosphorescence lifetime compared to NCDs@PVA, which is due to fluorine doping. This can be attributed to the role of the steric hindrance of C–F bonds in FNCDs-1 and hydrogen bonding, which results in the excellent photoluminescence stability of FNCDs-1@PVA. According to the phosphorescence lifetime curves of NCDs@PVA and FNCDs-1@PVA films, it is obvious that the phosphorescence lifetime of FNCDs-1@PVA can reach 494 ms. The afterglow visible to the naked eye can be up to 3 s, and the phosphorescence lifetime of NCDs@PVA is 473 ms. However, with the increase of the co-dopant reagents, the phosphorescence lifetime becomes shorter, and the phosphorescence lifetime of FNCDs-3@PVA is only 332 ms (Fig. 3(d)). The weakened phosphorescence lifetime of FNCDs-3 may be due to the lack of –CF2 bonds and the weaker strength of C–F bonds than that of FNCDs-1, whereas for NCDs, the strong CN/C–N bonding with n-π∗ electron transitions ensures the stable emission of phosphorescence. The comparison between FNCDs and NCDs demonstrates that the doping of fluorine atoms in the presence of the appropriate amount of doping reagent significantly affects the protection of RTP. The quantum yields (QY) of NCDs, FNCDs-1, and FNCDs-3 were tested to be 2.22 %, 5.29 %, and 6.27 %, respectively.

Furthermore, taking FNCDs-1@PVA as an example, we performed temperature, humidity and pH tests on RTP flexible films. The PVA film was dried at different temperatures, and the optimal phosphorescence intensity was found at 30 °C, which indicates the material is temperature selective (Fig. S8). In addition, the humidity response test showed that the FNCDs-1@PVA responded selectively to water vapor, as evidenced by the fact that the fluorescence intensity of the film increased with time under water vapor conditions, but the phosphorescence was easily quenched by water vapor (Fig. S9). This is because the PVA matrix may swell upon hygroscopicity, which contributes to a more homogeneous dispersion of CDs in the matrix and reduces the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, thus increasing the fluorescence intensity. However, water can break the hydrogen-bonding interactions between the CDs and the PVA, resulting in phosphorescence quenching. Therefore, after the film has been atomized by water vapor, the phenomenon of phosphorescence is almost impossible to observe with the naked eye. The option of re-drying the film at 30 °C restores the fluorescence intensity to its original effect, and the green phosphorescence reappears. It proves the reusability of this FNCDs-1@PVA flexible film, which reduces the waste of resources to a certain extent and aligns with the concept of sustainable development. Then, the pH stimulation response of the film was explored. In the fluorescence spectroscopy test, the films were subjected to the saturated vapor of hydrochloric acid for 10 s, 30 s, and then subsequently to the saturated vapor of ammonia for 40 s under optimal excitation wavelength (ex = 340 nm). The results show that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases from 0 s to 10 s–30 s when the film is subjected to saturated acid vapor. However, the greatly improved after the film was exposed to saturated alkali vapor for 40 s (Fig. 3(e)). The phosphorescence spectra of the three films were measured under 270 nm excitation by exposing the films again to the same conditions as tested above. It was found that the change in phosphorescence intensity was similar to the change in fluorescence intensity, both of which experienced a decrease under hydrochloric acid vapor and an increase under ammonia vapor, which could be assigned to protonation and deprotonation (Fig. 3(f)). Under saturated acid vapor conditions, although many intra- and inter-dot hydrogen bonds between amide/amino-type N atoms and F atoms existed on the CDs to prevent the quenching of triplet excitons, the protonation occurring in the amide/amino-type N atoms and the CN bonds led to the dissociation of the hydrogen bonds, which lead to the quenching effect of oxygen on phosphorescence increased. In contrast, hydrogen bond synthesis occurred under alkaline conditions, reducing oxygen penetration and enhancing fluorescence/phosphorescence. Table S1 compares the optical properties of different RTP CDs. To better understand the RTP properties of FNCDs, the measured phosphorescence spectra of FNCDs-1@PVA showed that the optimal excitation wavelength was located at 270 nm, indicating that the phosphorescence mainly originated from the n-π∗ electron transitions of the CN/C–N bond and that the fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of FNCDs-1@PVA indicated the presence of a Stokes shift (Fig. 3(g)). Based on the above data, the room-temperature phosphorescent flexible film responds to humidity, temperature and pH conditions. Its properties can be applied to information encryption and anti-counterfeiting with research significance.

Triple-state excitons are composed of spin-parallel electron-hole pairs, and small ISCs promote spin-orbit coupling, causing CDs to emit phosphorescence [49]. The energy of the triplet excitons transits back to the respective vibrational energy levels of the ground state in the form of radiation; vibrations/rotations of the molecules also dissipated energy (Fig. 3(h)). Therefore, the generation and protection of triplet excitons are necessary to realize phosphorescence. We achieve the protection of triplet excitons by using PVA as a carrier of CDs, which is rich in hydroxyl groups that create hydrogen bonds with CDs to inhibit intramolecular vibration/rotation and also isolate the CDs to reduce the electron/energy transfer between neighboring CDs, thus the energy of the triplet excitons from being dissipated as much as possible. Interestingly, when we doped the RTP CDs with N and F atoms, the phosphorescence lifetime of CDs@PVA was significantly enhanced. In the multi-conjugated system, the introduction of electron-withdrawing F atoms leads to the red-shift of fluorescence, which may be attributed to the fact that the F atoms modulate the electronic states and conformations on the surface of the CDs, narrowing the energy gap between S1 and T1 and lowering the energy levels [50]. In addition, the heavy atom effect can effectively improve the ISC efficiency of CDs. According to the XPS spectra shown, due to the presence of F atoms, there is a certain amount of ionic and semi-ionic C–F bonds in CDs, which is conducive to the formation of hydrogen bonding in the surrounding water, the polymer matrix, and thus inhibit the quenching of triplet state excitons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the responsive properties of CDs, we try to demonstrate their potential applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. Table S2 compares the doping methods and applications of different CDs. Firstly, the anti-counterfeiting encryption strategy with the combination of the program flow chart and afterglow lifetime. Different CDs@PVA are assembled into Arabic numerals. The transformation of colors and numbers is used as variables so that the final output of "3, Green" is correct and the rest of the information is wrong (Fig. 4 (a)). Secondly, ordinary barcode scanning alone can no longer prevent information leakage. We combine Morse code and barcode encryption as a new method (Fig. S10). FNCDs-1@PVA and FNCDs-3@PVA films are placed on top of each other, and no information can be read when the UV light is turned on. However, when the UV light is turned off, FNCDs-1@PVA emits a continuous green phosphorescent barcode under dark conditions. At this time, the mobile phone scans the code to obtain information. we set 0 for “.”, 1 for “-”, 2 for space, using Morse code to the information obtained by the second conversion, and finally decrypted to get "CHEM". In emergency conditions, the intelligence officer can use water vapor to quickly "Fake Destroy" the information so that the phosphorescence quenches; after the danger, the film can be heated to 30 °C, 1 h to restore the phosphorescence, and re-access to information.

In the field of optical material information encryption, the combination of computer algorithms and materials is considered an advanced encryption strategy. Acid vapor and alkali vapor have little effect on the phosphorescence intensity of FNCDs-1@PVA and FNCDs-3@PVA films under naked-eye observation. On this basis, we borrowed the RAS digital signature strategy for computers to identify the authenticity of the information by the transmission of information such as public key, private key, document, and signature between person A and B (Fig. 4(b)). The document is generated by arranging FNCDs-1@PVA and FNCDs-3@PVA acts as a jamming message to protect the document. The file is unlocked by a specific public key to get the message "2467". A operates on the file with private key 1 to generate a specific signature, "4689". Subsequently, the document and the signature will be transmitted to B. Only when B uses the private key 2 to restore the signature to the document "2467″ and uses the public key to decrypt the document with the message "2467″, that is, the document and the signature be transformed into the same information by B, can it be proved that the document has not been tampered. The information cannot be easily destroyed because of the film's good stability in acid or alkaline environments. This strategy ensures the accuracy of the information to a certain extent, improves the security coefficient of the encrypted information, and has good application prospects.

Taking this as inspiration, we used CDs@PVA to create dynamic binary and quaternary interconversion encryption patterns, added the decryption idea of Rubik's Cube and divided the whole process into encryption and decryption parts. The set of patterns can present different messages over time under dark conditions. We define the non-phosphorescence as 0 and the phosphorescence as 1. Through the difference in the phosphorescence lifetime indicated by R1, R2 and R3, we get RR1 +R2 +R3. Then, FNCD-3, NCD, and FNCD-1 are designated as 1, 2, and 3, respectively. In addition, the pure PVA film has no phosphorescence; thus, it is 0. At this point, the program-coded information encryption model is initially constructed. Taking "NEFU" as an example, the letters are converted to binary by ASCII, and the binary code of four letters N, E, F, and U is obtained. Then, we convert them to quaternary to get a set of quaternary codes. In the next step, the CD@PVA and the resulting quaternary numbers are arranged orderly, and the film containing the information is attached to a second-order Rubik's Cube. With the strong encryption effect of the Rubik's Cube and its ability to effectively prevent all kinds of cryptographic attacks, a three-dimensional "encryption box" is formed, and the encryption process is completed. (Fig. 5 (a)). After the UV lamp is turned off, record the phosphorescence pattern and get three sets of numbers under the time of 0 s, 1 s, and 3 s, respectively, i.e., R1, R2, and R3. Then, use RR1 +R2 +R3 to obtain the quaternary information, convert it back to binary and finally get the encrypted information "NEFU"; the decryption process is over. (Fig. 5(b)). However, reading information must be correctly directed and ordered before decryption begins. The decryption method mentioned in this paper starts with the green side. Then it reads the information from left to right, bottom to top, clockwise (green, blue, white, yellow), and the orange and red sides are interfering information. This shows that only the correct way of transforming the information can get the required information. Otherwise, there will be much incorrect information. This demonstrates that the strategy has a great guarantee for the security of information and has a large information storage capacity, which solves the problem of easy theft of information and insufficient information storage capacity.

In summary, it is a simple, green strategy for synthesizing high-performance CDs using renewable sodium lignosulfonate as a carbon source. CDs emit phosphorescence by doping with F and N atoms and embedding them in PVA to make flexible phosphorescent films. This is attributed to the intra- and inter-dot hydrogen bonding between PVA and CDs, which narrows the difference in energy level and thus facilitates spin-orbit coupling. For FNCDs@PVA, in addition to the role of PVA, the FNCDs have ionic and semi-ionic C–F bonds, which facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds in the surrounding water, the polymer matrix. At the same time, the formation of C–F bonds provides steric hindrance, which inhibits the quenching of triplet-state excitons. In this paper, we have successfully applied flexible RTP CDs films to information encryption and anti-counterfeiting because of their multi-conditional response (e.g., temperature, humidity, pH) properties. A series of encryption and anti-counterfeiting strategies, from simple to complex, was designed using various algorithms in combination with time, light color and chemical stimuli. The preparation of encryption and anti-counterfeiting materials was achieved, proving that fluorine-nitrogen co-doped RTP CD films have great potential in encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

Chenyang Zhang: Investigation. Jiandong Guo: Methodology. Fanwei Kong: Visualization. Shuqi Zou: Software. Mingyang Wu: Methodology. Jinsong Peng: Validation. Xiaobai Li: Supervision. Chunxia Chen: Project administration. Hongwei Ma: Super.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2024B003), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62205052, 22374017), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572023CT12).
